speaker: BEROWNE
text: The King, he is hunting the deer; I am

speaker: BEROWNE
text: coursing myself. They have pitched a toil; I am

speaker: BEROWNE
text: toiling in a pitch—pitch that defiles. Defile! A foul

speaker: BEROWNE
text: word. Well, “set thee down, sorrow”; for so they

speaker: BEROWNE
text: say the fool said, and so say I, and I the fool. Well

speaker: BEROWNE
text: proved, wit. By the Lord, this love is as mad as Ajax.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: It kills sheep, it kills me, I a sheep. Well proved

speaker: BEROWNE
text: again, o’ my side. I will not love. If I do, hang me. I’

speaker: BEROWNE
text: faith, I will not. O, but her eye! By this light, but for

speaker: BEROWNE
text: her eye I would not love her; yes, for her two eyes.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Well, I do nothing in the world but lie, and lie in my

speaker: BEROWNE
text: throat. By heaven, I do love, and it hath taught me to

speaker: BEROWNE
text: rhyme, and to be melancholy. And here is part of my

speaker: BEROWNE
text: rhyme, and here my melancholy. Well, she hath one

speaker: BEROWNE
text: o’ my sonnets already. The clown bore it, the fool

speaker: BEROWNE
text: sent it, and the lady hath it. Sweet clown, sweeter

speaker: BEROWNE
text: fool, sweetest lady. By the world, I would not care a

speaker: BEROWNE
text: pin, if the other three were in. Here comes one with

speaker: BEROWNE
text: a paper. God give him grace to groan.

speaker: KING
text: Ay me!

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Shot, by heaven! Proceed, sweet

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Cupid. Thou hast thumped him with thy birdbolt

speaker: BEROWNE
text: under the left pap. In faith, secrets!

speaker: KING
text: So sweet a kiss the golden sun gives not

speaker: KING
text: To those fresh morning drops upon the rose

speaker: KING
text: As thy eyebeams, when their fresh rays have smote

speaker: KING
text: The night of dew that on my cheeks down flows.

speaker: KING
text: Nor shines the silver moon one-half so bright

speaker: KING
text: Through the transparent bosom of the deep

speaker: KING
text: As doth thy face, through tears of mine, give light.

speaker: KING
text: Thou shin’st in every tear that I do weep.

speaker: KING
text: No drop but as a coach doth carry thee;

speaker: KING
text: So ridest thou triumphing in my woe.

speaker: KING
text: Do but behold the tears that swell in me,

speaker: KING
text: And they thy glory through my grief will show.

speaker: KING
text: But do not love thyself; then thou wilt keep

speaker: KING
text: My tears for glasses, and still make me weep.

speaker: KING
text: O queen of queens, how far dost thou excel

speaker: KING
text: No thought can think, nor tongue of mortal tell.

speaker: KING
text: How shall she know my griefs? I’ll drop the paper.

speaker: KING
text: Sweet leaves, shade folly. Who is he comes here?

speaker: KING
text: What, Longaville, and reading! Listen, ear.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Now, in thy likeness, one more fool appear!

speaker: LONGAVILLE
text: Ay me! I am forsworn.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Why, he comes in like a perjure, wearing papers!

speaker: KING
text: In love, I hope! Sweet fellowship in shame.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: One drunkard loves another of the name.

speaker: LONGAVILLE
text: Am I the first that have been perjured so?

speaker: BEROWNE
text: I could put thee in comfort: not by two that I know.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Thou makest the triumviry, the corner-cap of society,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: The shape of love’s Tyburn, that hangs up simplicity.

speaker: LONGAVILLE
text: I fear these stubborn lines lack power to move.

speaker: LONGAVILLE
text:
Reads
                .
                 
              
                O
                 
                sweet
                 
                Maria
                ,
                 
                empress
                 
                of
                 
                my
                 
                love
                —

speaker: LONGAVILLE
text: These numbers will I tear and write in prose.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: O, rhymes are guards on wanton Cupid’s hose.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Disfigure not his shop!

speaker: LONGAVILLE
text: This same shall go.

speaker: LONGAVILLE
text: Did not the heavenly rhetoric of thine eye,

speaker: LONGAVILLE
text: ’Gainst whom the world cannot hold argument,

speaker: LONGAVILLE
text: Persuade my heart to this false perjury?

speaker: LONGAVILLE
text: Vows for thee broke deserve not punishment.

speaker: LONGAVILLE
text: A woman I forswore, but I will prove,

speaker: LONGAVILLE
text: Thou being a goddess, I forswore not thee.

speaker: LONGAVILLE
text: My vow was earthly, thou a heavenly love.

speaker: LONGAVILLE
text: Thy grace being gained cures all disgrace in me.

speaker: LONGAVILLE
text: Vows are but breath, and breath a vapor is.

speaker: LONGAVILLE
text: Then thou, fair sun, which on my Earth dost shine,

speaker: LONGAVILLE
text: Exhal’st this vapor-vow; in thee it is.

speaker: LONGAVILLE
text: If broken, then, it is no fault of mine.

speaker: LONGAVILLE
text: If by me broke, what fool is not so wise

speaker: LONGAVILLE
text: To lose an oath to win a paradise?

speaker: BEROWNE
text: This is the liver vein, which makes flesh a deity,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: A green goose a goddess. Pure, pure idolatry.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: God amend us, God amend. We are much out o’ th’ way.

speaker: LONGAVILLE
text: By whom shall I send this?—Company? Stay.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: All hid, all hid—an old infant play.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Like a demigod here sit I in the sky,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: And wretched fools’ secrets heedfully o’ereye.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: More sacks to the mill. O heavens, I have my wish.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Dumaine transformed! Four woodcocks in a dish.

speaker: DUMAINE
text: O most divine Kate!

speaker: BEROWNE
text: O most profane coxcomb!

speaker: DUMAINE
text: By heaven, the wonder in a mortal eye!

speaker: BEROWNE
text: By Earth, she is not, corporal. There you lie.

speaker: DUMAINE
text: Her amber hairs for foul hath amber quoted.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: An amber-colored raven was well noted.

speaker: DUMAINE
text: As upright as the cedar.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Stoop, I say.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Her shoulder is with child.

speaker: DUMAINE
text: As fair as day.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Ay, as some days, but then no sun must shine.

speaker: DUMAINE
text: O, that I had my wish!

speaker: LONGAVILLE
text: And I had mine!

speaker: KING
text: And mine too, good Lord!

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Amen, so I had mine. Is not that a good word?

speaker: DUMAINE
text: I would forget her, but a fever she

speaker: DUMAINE
text: Reigns in my blood, and will remembered be.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: A fever in your blood? Why, then incision

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Would let her out in saucers! Sweet misprision.

speaker: DUMAINE
text: Once more I’ll read the ode that I have writ.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Once more I’ll mark how love can vary wit.

speaker: DUMAINE
text: On a day—alack the day!—

speaker: DUMAINE
text: Love, whose month is ever May,

speaker: DUMAINE
text: Spied a blossom passing fair,

speaker: DUMAINE
text: Playing in the wanton air.

speaker: DUMAINE
text: Through the velvet leaves the wind,

speaker: DUMAINE
text: All unseen, can passage find;

speaker: DUMAINE
text: That the lover, sick to death,

speaker: DUMAINE
text: Wished himself the heaven’s breath.

speaker: DUMAINE
text: “Air,” quoth he, “thy cheeks may blow.

speaker: DUMAINE
text: Air, would I might triumph so!”

speaker: DUMAINE
text: But, alack, my hand is sworn

speaker: DUMAINE
text: Ne’er to pluck thee from thy thorn.

speaker: DUMAINE
text: Vow, alack, for youth unmeet,

speaker: DUMAINE
text: Youth so apt to pluck a sweet.

speaker: DUMAINE
text: Do not call it sin in me

speaker: DUMAINE
text: That I am forsworn for thee—

speaker: DUMAINE
text: Thou for whom Jove would swear

speaker: DUMAINE
text: Juno but an Ethiope were,

speaker: DUMAINE
text: And deny himself for Jove,

speaker: DUMAINE
text: Turning mortal for thy love.

speaker: DUMAINE
text: This will I send, and something else more plain

speaker: DUMAINE
text: That shall express my true love’s fasting pain.

speaker: DUMAINE
text: O, would the King, Berowne, and Longaville

speaker: DUMAINE
text: Were lovers too! Ill to example ill

speaker: DUMAINE
text: Would from my forehead wipe a perjured note,

speaker: DUMAINE
text: For none offend where all alike do dote.

speaker: LONGAVILLE
text: Dumaine, thy love is far from charity,

speaker: LONGAVILLE
text: That in love’s grief desir’st society.

speaker: LONGAVILLE
text: You may look pale, but I should blush, I know,

speaker: LONGAVILLE
text: To be o’er-heard and taken napping so.

speaker: KING
text:
To
                 
                Longaville
                .
                 
              Come, sir, you blush! As his, your case is such.

speaker: KING
text: You chide at him, offending twice as much.

speaker: KING
text: You do not love Maria? Longaville

speaker: KING
text: Did never sonnet for her sake compile,

speaker: KING
text: Nor never lay his wreathèd arms athwart

speaker: KING
text: His loving bosom to keep down his heart?

speaker: KING
text: I have been closely shrouded in this bush

speaker: KING
text: And marked you both, and for you both did blush.

speaker: KING
text: I heard your guilty rhymes, observed your fashion,

speaker: KING
text: Saw sighs reek from you, noted well your passion.

speaker: KING
text: “Ay, me!” says one. “O Jove!” the other cries.

speaker: KING
text: One, her hairs were gold, crystal the other’s eyes.

speaker: KING
text:
To
                 
                Longaville
                .
                 
              You would for paradise break faith and troth,

speaker: KING
text:
To
                 
                Dumaine
                .
                 
              And Jove, for your love, would infringe an oath.

speaker: KING
text: What will Berowne say when that he shall hear

speaker: KING
text: Faith infringed, which such zeal did swear?

speaker: KING
text: How will he scorn, how will he spend his wit!

speaker: KING
text: How will he triumph, leap, and laugh at it!

speaker: KING
text: For all the wealth that ever I did see,

speaker: KING
text: I would not have him know so much by me.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Now step I forth to whip hypocrisy.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Ah, good my liege, I pray thee pardon me.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Good heart, what grace hast thou thus to reprove

speaker: BEROWNE
text: These worms for loving, that art most in love?

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Your eyes do make no coaches; in your tears

speaker: BEROWNE
text: There is no certain princess that appears.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: You’ll not be perjured, ’tis a hateful thing!

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Tush, none but minstrels like of sonneting!

speaker: BEROWNE
text: But are you not ashamed? Nay, are you not,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: All three of you, to be thus much o’ershot?

speaker: BEROWNE
text:
To
                 
                Longaville
                .
                 
              You found his mote, the King your mote did see,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: But I a beam do find in each of three.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: O, what a scene of fool’ry have I seen,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Of sighs, of groans, of sorrow, and of teen!

speaker: BEROWNE
text: O me, with what strict patience have I sat,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: To see a king transformèd to a gnat!

speaker: BEROWNE
text: To see great Hercules whipping a gig,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: And profound Solomon to tune a jig,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: And Nestor play at pushpin with the boys,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: And critic Timon laugh at idle toys.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Where lies thy grief, O tell me, good Dumaine?

speaker: BEROWNE
text: And gentle Longaville, where lies thy pain?

speaker: BEROWNE
text: And where my liege’s? All about the breast!

speaker: BEROWNE
text: A caudle, ho!

speaker: KING
text: Too bitter is thy jest.

speaker: KING
text: Are we betrayed thus to thy overview?

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Not you to me, but I betrayed by you.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: I, that am honest, I, that hold it sin

speaker: BEROWNE
text: To break the vow I am engagèd in.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: I am betrayed by keeping company

speaker: BEROWNE
text: With men like you, men of inconstancy.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: When shall you see me write a thing in rhyme?

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Or groan for Joan? or spend a minute’s time

speaker: BEROWNE
text: In pruning me? When shall you hear that I

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Will praise a hand, a foot, a face, an eye,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: A gait, a state, a brow, a breast, a waist,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: A leg, a limb—

speaker: KING
text: Soft, whither away so fast?

speaker: KING
text: A true man, or a thief, that gallops so?

speaker: BEROWNE
text: I post from love. Good lover, let me go.

speaker: JAQUENETTA
text: God bless the King.

speaker: KING
text: What present hast thou there?

speaker: COSTARD
text: Some certain treason.

speaker: KING
text: What makes treason here?

speaker: COSTARD
text: Nay, it makes nothing, sir.

speaker: KING
text: If it mar nothing neither,

speaker: KING
text: The treason and you go in peace away together.

speaker: JAQUENETTA
text: I beseech your Grace, let this letter be read.

speaker: JAQUENETTA
text: Our person misdoubts it. ’Twas treason, he said.

speaker: KING
text: Berowne, read it over.

speaker: KING
text: Where hadst thou it?

speaker: JAQUENETTA
text: Of Costard.

speaker: KING
text: Where hadst thou it?

speaker: COSTARD
text: Of Dun Adramadio, Dun Adramadio.

speaker: KING
text: How now, what is in you? Why dost thou tear it?

speaker: BEROWNE
text: A toy, my liege, a toy. Your Grace needs not fear it.

speaker: LONGAVILLE
text: It did move him to passion, and therefore let’s hear it.

speaker: DUMAINE
text: It is Berowne’s writing, and here is his name.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Ah, you whoreson loggerhead, you were born to do me shame.—

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Guilty, my lord, guilty. I confess, I confess.

speaker: KING
text: What?

speaker: BEROWNE
text: That you three fools lacked me fool to make up the mess.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: He, he, and you—and you, my liege—and I

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Are pickpurses in love, and we deserve to die.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: O, dismiss this audience, and I shall tell you more.

speaker: DUMAINE
text: Now the number is even.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: True, true, we are four.

speaker: BEROWNE
text:
Pointing
                 
                to
                 
                Jaquenetta
                 
                and
                 
                Costard
                .
                 
              Will these turtles be gone?

speaker: KING
text: Hence, sirs. Away.

speaker: COSTARD
text: Walk aside the true folk, and let the traitors stay.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Sweet lords, sweet lovers, O, let us embrace.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: As true we are as flesh and blood can be.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: The sea will ebb and flow, heaven show his face;

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Young blood doth not obey an old decree.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: We cannot cross the cause why we were born;

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Therefore of all hands must we be forsworn.

speaker: KING
text: What, did these rent lines show some love of thine?

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Did they, quoth you? Who sees the heavenly Rosaline

speaker: BEROWNE
text: That, like a rude and savage man of Ind

speaker: BEROWNE
text: At the first op’ning of the gorgeous East,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Bows not his vassal head and, strucken blind,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Kisses the base ground with obedient breast?

speaker: BEROWNE
text: What peremptory eagle-sighted eye

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Dares look upon the heaven of her brow

speaker: BEROWNE
text: That is not blinded by her majesty?

speaker: KING
text: What zeal, what fury, hath inspired thee now?

speaker: KING
text: My love, her mistress, is a gracious moon,

speaker: KING
text: She an attending star scarce seen a light.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: My eyes are then no eyes, nor I Berowne.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: O, but for my love, day would turn to night!

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Of all complexions the culled sovereignty

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Do meet as at a fair in her fair cheek.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Where several worthies make one dignity,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Where nothing wants that want itself doth seek.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Lend me the flourish of all gentle tongues—

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Fie, painted rhetoric! O, she needs it not!

speaker: BEROWNE
text: To things of sale a seller’s praise belongs.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: She passes praise. Then praise too short doth blot.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: A withered hermit, fivescore winters worn,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Might shake off fifty, looking in her eye.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Beauty doth varnish age, as if newborn,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: And gives the crutch the cradle’s infancy.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: O, ’tis the sun that maketh all things shine!

speaker: KING
text: By heaven, thy love is black as ebony.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Is ebony like her? O word divine!

speaker: BEROWNE
text: A wife of such wood were felicity.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: O, who can give an oath? Where is a book,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: That I may swear beauty doth beauty lack

speaker: BEROWNE
text: If that she learn not of her eye to look?

speaker: BEROWNE
text: No face is fair that is not full so black.

speaker: KING
text: O, paradox! Black is the badge of hell,

speaker: KING
text: The hue of dungeons and the school of night,

speaker: KING
text: And beauty’s crest becomes the heavens well.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Devils soonest tempt, resembling spirits of light.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: O, if in black my lady’s brows be decked,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: It mourns that painting and usurping hair

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Should ravish doters with a false aspect:

speaker: BEROWNE
text: And therefore is she born to make black fair.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Her favor turns the fashion of the days,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: For native blood is counted painting now.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: And therefore red, that would avoid dispraise,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Paints itself black to imitate her brow.

speaker: DUMAINE
text: To look like her are chimney-sweepers black.

speaker: LONGAVILLE
text: And since her time are colliers counted bright.

speaker: KING
text: And Ethiopes of their sweet complexion crack.

speaker: DUMAINE
text: Dark needs no candles now, for dark is light.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Your mistresses dare never come in rain,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: For fear their colors should be washed away.

speaker: KING
text: ’Twere good yours did, for, sir, to tell you plain,

speaker: KING
text: I’ll find a fairer face not washed today.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: I’ll prove her fair, or talk till doomsday here.

speaker: KING
text: No devil will fright thee then so much as she.

speaker: DUMAINE
text: I never knew man hold vile stuff so dear.

speaker: LONGAVILLE
text: Look, here’s thy love; my foot and her face see.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: O, if the streets were pavèd with thine eyes.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Her feet were much too dainty for such tread.

speaker: DUMAINE
text: O vile! Then as she goes, what upward lies

speaker: DUMAINE
text: The street should see as she walked overhead.

speaker: KING
text: But what of this? Are we not all in love?

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Nothing so sure, and thereby all forsworn.

speaker: KING
text: Then leave this chat, and, good Berowne, now prove

speaker: KING
text: Our loving lawful, and our faith not torn.

speaker: DUMAINE
text: Ay, marry, there, some flattery for this evil.

speaker: LONGAVILLE
text: O, some authority how to proceed,

speaker: LONGAVILLE
text: Some tricks, some quillets, how to cheat the devil.

speaker: DUMAINE
text: Some salve for perjury.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: O, ’tis more than need.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Have at you, then, affection’s men-at-arms!

speaker: BEROWNE
text: O, we have made a vow to study, lords,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: And in that vow we have forsworn our books.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: For when would you, my liege, or you, or you,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: In leaden contemplation have found out

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Such fiery numbers as the prompting eyes

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Of beauty’s tutors have enriched you with?

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Other slow arts entirely keep the brain

speaker: BEROWNE
text: And therefore, finding barren practicers,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Scarce show a harvest of their heavy toil.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: But love, first learnèd in a lady’s eyes,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Lives not alone immurèd in the brain,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: But with the motion of all elements

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Courses as swift as thought in every power,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: And gives to every power a double power,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Above their functions and their offices.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: It adds a precious seeing to the eye.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: A lover’s eyes will gaze an eagle blind.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: A lover’s ear will hear the lowest sound,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: When the suspicious head of theft is stopped.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Love’s feeling is more soft and sensible

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Than are the tender horns of cockled snails.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Love’s tongue proves dainty Bacchus gross in taste.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: For valor, is not love a Hercules,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Still climbing trees in the Hesperides?

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Subtle as Sphinx, as sweet and musical

speaker: BEROWNE
text: As bright Apollo’s lute strung with his hair.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: And when love speaks, the voice of all the gods

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Make heaven drowsy with the harmony.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Never durst poet touch a pen to write

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Until his ink were tempered with love’s sighs.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: O, then his lines would ravish savage ears

speaker: BEROWNE
text: And plant in tyrants mild humility.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: From women’s eyes this doctrine I derive.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: They sparkle still the right Promethean fire.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: They are the books, the arts, the academes

speaker: BEROWNE
text: That show, contain, and nourish all the world.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Else none at all in ought proves excellent.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Then fools you were these women to forswear,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Or, keeping what is sworn, you will prove fools.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: For wisdom’s sake, a word that all men love,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Or for love’s sake, a word that loves all men,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Or for men’s sake, the authors of these women,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Or women’s sake, by whom we men are men,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Let us once lose our oaths to find ourselves,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Or else we lose ourselves to keep our oaths.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: It is religion to be thus forsworn,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: For charity itself fulfills the law,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: And who can sever love from charity?

speaker: KING
text: Saint Cupid, then, and, soldiers, to the field!

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Advance your standards, and upon them, lords.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Pell-mell, down with them. But be first advised

speaker: BEROWNE
text: In conflict that you get the sun of them.

speaker: LONGAVILLE
text: Now to plain dealing. Lay these glozes by.

speaker: LONGAVILLE
text: Shall we resolve to woo these girls of France?

speaker: KING
text: And win them, too. Therefore let us devise

speaker: KING
text: Some entertainment for them in their tents.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: First, from the park let us conduct them thither.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Then homeward every man attach the hand

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Of his fair mistress. In the afternoon

speaker: BEROWNE
text: We will with some strange pastime solace them,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Such as the shortness of the time can shape;

speaker: BEROWNE
text: For revels, dances, masques, and merry hours

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Forerun fair love, strewing her way with flowers.

speaker: KING
text: Away, away! No time shall be omitted

speaker: KING
text: That will betime and may by us be fitted.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Allons! Allons! Sowed cockle reaped no corn,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: And justice always whirls in equal measure.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Light wenches may prove plagues to men forsworn;

speaker: BEROWNE
text: If so, our copper buys no better treasure.
